Just now

For decades, C has been the default language for systems programming. Operating systems, embedded software, databases, game engines, networking stacks, runtimes, compilers, and performance-critical infrastructure have all been built on top of C. Its success is not accidental: C gives programmers direct control over memory, layout, pointers, calling conventions, and runtime behavior.
But that control comes at a cost. C exposes the machine, but it also exposes developers to undefined behavior, manual resource management bugs, preprocessor complexity, fragile build systems, and security problems that modern software can no longer ignore. The question is no longer whether C is powerful. It clearly is. The better question is whether we can keep the useful parts of C while removing some of the historical baggage.
That is where Zig becomes interesting.
Zig is not trying to be a garbage-collected application language like Go. It is not trying to enforce memory safety through a borrow checker like Rust. It is also not merely “C with nicer syntax.” Zig is a systems programming language and toolchain designed around explicitness, compile-time execution, predictable performance, and smooth interoperability with C. The official Zig documentation describes it as a language and toolchain for…
